entities.description: A 1999 electronic encoding keyed from the 1988 Kraus reprint. Its declaration says that 'Chrisian' keyed the text to an assessed accuracy of one error per 20,000 characters; the identity and organizational type of that named keying contributor were not independently established. The encoding follows the print edition where possible but omits the Latin text and synoptic marginal gloss.

entities.description: The immediate print source identified by the U-M header: a 1988 Millwood, New York reprint of Horstmann's 1886 EETS edition. No evidence of textual revision or fresh collation was found; the source describes it simply as a reprint.

rights.basis: The underlying 1886 edition is public domain in the United States, but no copy of the 1988 reprint or item-specific notice was inspected to determine whether it added protectable modern matter.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum' by John of Hildesheim, Edited from the MSS., Together with the Latin Text, EETS OS 85 (London: N. Trubner & Co., 1886), xxi, 312 pp.

bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 85
bibliographic.title: The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum'

entities.description: Horstmann's parallel-text edition prints the Cambridge and Royal English versions on facing even and odd pages, English variant apparatus, readings from a Wynkyn de Worde print, readings from Cotton Vespasian E XVI, and a Latin text from page 206. The U-M encoding represents only a selection of this volume.
entities.label: Carl Horstmann's Three Kings of Cologne (EETS OS 85, 1886)

rights.basis: The inspected volume was published in London in 1886; U-M currently presents this exact item as public domain and assigns No Copyright-United States, while Internet Archive marks the scan NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT.
rights.component: Horstmann's 1886 edition, transcription, introduction, apparatus, and the underlying medieval texts
rights.jurisdiction: United States

bibliographic.citation: Frank Schaer, The Three Kings of Cologne: A Diplomatic Edition of the Unabridged English Version of John of Hildesheim's Historia Trium Regum in Durham MS Hunter 15, with a Reconstruction of the Translator's Latin Text on Facing Pages Based on Corpus Christi College Cambridge MS 275, and a Study of the Manuscript Tradition (PhD thesis, University of Adelaide, submitted 1992; degree 1993), 3 vols.

entities.description: A three-volume doctoral thesis providing a diplomatic edition and a detailed study of the English manuscript tradition. It is supporting scholarship, not an upstream source of the 1886 Horstmann edition or the U-M encoding.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.notes: The Faculty of English research note summarizes the Western Illuminated Manuscripts catalogue as dating the codex to the fifteenth century and citing either half as possible.
dateStatements.value: Fifteenth century; either half of the century has been proposed

entities.description: The principal Cambridge witness used for Horstmann's even-page English text. The current IMEP handlist locates the standard prose Three Kings of Cologne at part 1, fols. 1r-23v, followed in the codex by a Middle English Prose Brut. Horstmann reports that the first page was unreadable from blackening and supplied its text from Cotton Titus A XXV. U-M's SOURCEDESC writes 'Ee.4.2,' but its own table of contents and text heading, Horstmann's edition, and the current handlist establish the shelfmark as Ee.4.32.

holding.notes: The exact item is described in the published IMEP Cambridge handlist. CUL states that most medieval manuscripts remain cataloged primarily in printed catalogues rather than ArchiveSearch.

entities.label: Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32, part 1

physicalDescription.extent: The Three Kings of Cologne occupies separately foliated part 1, fols. 1r-23v
notes: The codex also contains the English Prose Brut; adjacent text is outside the cataloged work.

rights.basis: The selected-image research note does not provide an item-level reuse license, and CUL's imaging service distinguishes personal research scans from publication-quality images and permission.

access.accessMethod: Request by shelfmark under British Library reader-pass and Western Manuscripts consultation rules
access.entityLabel: British Library Royal MS 18 A X, item 15 (from fol. 87r)
notes: The current record requires a letter of introduction and consultation in London.

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: The first-group Royal recension printed by Horstmann on the odd pages of his parallel edition. The openings of chapters 1-32 spell the names Margareta Moningtown and Mawde Stranlea; Schaer's stemmatic analysis treats the Royal text as a deliberate secondary revision of the standard translation, not its original authorship signature.

historical_designations: British Museum Royal MS 18 A X

entities.label: British Library Royal MS 18 A X, item 15 (from fol. 87r)

physicalDescription.extent: Codex: i + 134 folios; The Three Kings of Cologne begins at fol. 87 and the following item begins at fol. 119v
notes: The Three Kings is item 15 in a twenty-item theological and devotional anthology.
An end at fol. 119r is inferred from the catalogue's statement that the following item begins at fol. 119v; the record does not give a separate explicit for item 15.

rights.basis: The exact catalogue record exposes no digitized-content link and gives no image-specific reuse grant.
rights.component: Any modern photographs or scans of Royal MS 18 A X
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse

dateStatements.label: manuscript production
dateStatements.value: First half of the fifteenth century

entities.description: A first-half-fifteenth-century witness of the standard prose translation. Horstmann supplied the unreadable opening page of the Cambridge text from this manuscript and cited it throughout the variant apparatus as T.

access.accessMethod: Request exact component by shelfmark under British Library reader-pass and Western Manuscripts consultation rules
access.entityLabel: British Library Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, fols. 38r-69v

entities.description: An imperfect mid-fifteenth-century witness closely related to the Royal recension. Horstmann printed its readings against the Royal text as a separate appendix; it ends at 'mete to,' corresponding to Royal page 153 line 24.

holding.notes: The exact component record contains several works; The Three Kings occupies only fols. 38r-69v.
holding.shelfmark: Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, ff. 38-85

identifiers.value: Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, ff. 38-85

entities.label: British Library Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, fols. 38r-69v

physicalDescription.extent: The imperfect Three Kings of Cologne occupies fols. 38r-69v

rights.basis: The exact component and parent catalogue records expose no digitized-content link and give no image-specific reuse grant.
rights.component: Any modern photographs or scans of Cotton MS Vespasian E XVI, fols. 38r-69v
rights.jurisdiction: United Kingdom and intended place of reuse

entities.description: An aggregate description of witnesses cited in the 1886 English apparatus beyond the separately modeled Cambridge, Royal, Titus, and Vespasian items: Bodleian Douce 301, British Library Harley 1704, the dated 1442 'Bedford' manuscript now BL Add MS 36983, Patrick Papers 43, Cambridge University Library Kk.1.3, and other readings at the scopes Horstmann states. It is not a claim that all had the same textual status or were used throughout every chapter.
entities.label: Horstmann's additional English collation witnesses

dateStatements.label: printing
dateStatements.value: c. 1499; date omitted in the copy described by Horstmann

entities.description: An early printed version of the standard prose translation, conventionally dated 1499 although the edition itself omits the date. Horstmann states that it follows the Cambridge text or a copy but alters freely; his separate readings occupy pages 186-198.
entities.label: Wynkyn de Worde's first edition of The Three Kings of Cologne (c. 1499)
notes: A current item-level record for the surviving copy used by Horstmann was not verified in this review, so no present holding is asserted.

dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.notes: The terminus post quem is Florentius of Wevelinghoven's appointment as bishop of Munster in 1364; John died in 1375. A more precise date remains speculative.

entities.description: The widely transmitted Latin prose work about the Biblical Magi, their later lives, and the translation of their relics. It is the source work translated and abridged in 3KCol, not a physical exemplar identified as the translator's exact copy.
entities.label: John of Hildesheim, Historia trium regum

next_steps: Request a written interpretation from U-M Library before redistributing the XML or a close derivative as a content provider.
Keep copyright status, repository licenses, and inherited provider terms separate in downstream publication decisions.
openQuestions.question: Does U-M's current No Copyright-United States statement supersede or otherwise modify the 1999 XML restriction against redistribution by commercial or not-for-profit content providers?

next_steps: Ask CUL Rare Books and Early Manuscripts staff whether an unindexed digital surrogate or fuller internal record exists.
Ask QUB whether MS 59/215 covers the complete codex and whether a complete digital transfer can be supplied through the Virtual Reading Room or reprographics service.
Request fols. 1r-23v through the appropriate imaging route and obtain written reuse terms before publication.
Consult the printed Western Illuminated Manuscripts catalogue directly to record the evidence behind its two alternative half-century datings.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete institutional digital surrogate or more precise current item-level catalog record available for Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32?

next_steps: Ask British Library Western Manuscripts Reference Services about existing surrogates for the exact folio ranges.
Request written image-supply and publication terms rather than inferring permission from the public catalog records.
openQuestions.question: Do complete digital images of Royal MS 18 A X, Cotton Titus A XXV, or Cotton Vespasian E XVI exist behind unavailable legacy services or by mediated request, and what terms govern reuse?

next_steps: Map every bracket, note, and apparatus siglum to the corresponding witness and compare against page images.
Use Schaer's stemmatic apparatus to distinguish Horstmann's supplies, emendations, and inherited witness readings.
openQuestions.question: Which exact words and passages in Horstmann's Cambridge and Royal printed texts derive from each auxiliary witness rather than the named principal manuscript?

next_steps: Ask U-M whether project files identify the vendor or person and preserve keying, correction, or QA logs.
Do not silently normalize the name to 'Christian' without documentary support.
openQuestions.question: Who or what was 'Chrisian,' the keying contributor named in the 1999 U-M encoding declaration, and what surviving production or quality-control records document the electronic text?

reviewNotes: The witness graph is intentionally limited to the two directly transcribed texts, the separately evidenced Titus supply and Vespasian apparatus witnesses, and an aggregate description of Horstmann's remaining collation set; it is not a census of the more than twenty known witnesses.

U-M's SOURCEDESC uses the forms 'Ee.4.2' and 'Royal MS. 18.A.10'; its own table of contents and text headings, the edition, and the institutional descriptions establish the cataloged shelfmarks as Ee.4.32 and Royal MS 18 A X, which are used here.
No complete public manuscript facsimile was verified for Ee.4.32, Royal 18 A X, Cotton Titus A XXV, or Cotton Vespasian E XVI during this review.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of U-M's 1999 digital encoding, keyed from the 1988 Kraus reprint of Carl Horstmann's 1886 EETS edition. Horstmann printed Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32 and British Library Royal MS 18 A X in parallel, supplied the damaged Cambridge opening from Cotton Titus A XXV, and added apparatus from a wider English manuscript and print tradition. U-M reorganized the parallel texts into separate divisions, retained the English variants, and omitted Horstmann's Latin text and marginal synopsis. The graph keeps the anonymous Middle English translation, digital encoding, reprint, edition, witnesses, and digital scan distinct.

catalogSummary.title: The Three Kings of Cologne

catalogingSubject.label: The Three Kings of Cologne
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous standard Middle English prose translation and abridgment of the Historia trium regum attributed to John of Hildesheim, including the related recensions represented by Cambridge University Library MS Ee.4.32 and British Library Royal MS 18 A X. The cataloged work is distinct from John's Latin source work, the physical manuscript witnesses, Horstmann's 1886 parallel-text edition, its 1988 Kraus reprint, U-M's 1999 electronic encoding, and the repository XML artifact. It is also distinct from the independent Lambeth prose translation and the Middle English verse version that share forms of the same modern title.

contentParts.label: The Three Kings of Cologne

locators.value: begins fol. 87r; inferred through fol. 119r because the next item begins fol. 119v
parallel English texts on pp. 2-158; variants on pp. 159-205

notes: The Cambridge and Royal recensions are locators and textual versions of the whole, not intrinsic content parts to which one witness's date or dialect should be generalized.
contentParts.summary: The complete standard Middle English prose translation, represented in the source chain by a Cambridge text and a separately revised Royal recension.

assertion.notes: The broad normalized interval records the hard termini, not an assertion that translation occupied that whole period. Schaer accepts Horstmann's conventional date around 1400 but stresses that the linguistic basis is not decisive.

dateStatements.display: Around 1400; securely after the Latin source (1364-1375) and before the dated 1442 English witness
notes: Do not substitute the fifteenth-century witness dates or the 1886, 1988, and 1999 edition and encoding dates for the work's translation date.

extent.description: One prose translation represented by two edited witness texts. The XML contains 78 DIV2 nodes: 38 under the Cambridge division (35 tagged chapter and 3 tagged section) and 40 U-M-supplied sections under the Royal division. It also contains 7 DIV1 nodes (contents, introduction, two manuscript-text divisions, and three variant-apparatus divisions), 225 PB elements, and 46 MILESTONE elements. These are encoding metrics, not a stable intrinsic chapter count; U-M explicitly supplied the Royal headings and some section boundaries.

formStatements.description: A prose translation and abridgment; Latin quotations and rubrics do not change the work's discovery form.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: The XML's German, Dutch, French, Danish, Spanish, and Italian language declarations describe Horstmann's introduction and apparatus, not substantive whole-work languages of the cataloged Middle English translation.

scope.description: Biblical phrases, rubrics, citations, and source-text quotations embedded within the Middle English translation

notes: The manifest describes the standard prose translation across its evidenced recensions while binding only the witnesses and edition layers directly relevant to the 3KCol source chain.
Witness dates, foliation, dialect, and image terms remain scoped to lineage entities and are not generalized to the abstract work.

next_steps: Compare current LALME and manuscript-localization work across the earliest independent witnesses rather than transferring one copy's dialect to the translation.
Review post-Schaer scholarship for new codicological or source-text termini.
openQuestions.question: Can the date and place of the archetypal standard prose translation be narrowed beyond the conventional date around 1400 and a broad English origin?

next_steps: Use Schaer's stemma and apparatus to compare readings at the level of individual passages.
Do not equate Horstmann's Cambridge text, his Royal text, or the Durham unabridged witness with the archetype without qualification.
openQuestions.question: What precise form of the standard prose translation is closest to the archetype across the second-, third-, and Royal-recension branches?

notes: No exact place of translation is asserted. Witness dialects and copying places do not establish the place where the archetypal translation was made.
No evidence supports attributing the standard prose translation to John of Hildesheim or to a named English translator.

responsibilities.agentName: Anonymous Middle English abridger and adapter

assertion.notes: Schaer argues that the abridged form was primary and that the surviving unabridged form is a later expansion, while leaving aspects of the prototype abridgment unresolved.

responsibilities.display_name: Anonymous Middle English abridger and adapter

scope.description: The standard translation's archetypal abridgment of the Latin source

notes: The responsibility is source-work authorship and does not assign the anonymous Middle English translation to John.

scope.description: Authorship of the Latin source work translated and adapted in The Three Kings of Cologne
The immediate 1886 source edition and its 1988 reprint
Parallel English texts from Cambridge Ee.4.32 and Royal 18 A X, with a supplied opening and editorial interventions
Variant apparatus comparing additional manuscript witnesses and the early Wynkyn de Worde print

Lives, deaths, relics, and translations of the Three Kings
Epiphany devotion and the cult of the Three Kings
Medieval representations of Eastern geography, peoples, religions, and customs
notes: The work's medieval ethnographic claims are cataloged as subject matter, not endorsed as historical descriptions.

subjects.label: Prester John and Eastern Christian communities

summaries.value: An anonymous Middle English prose translation and abridgment of John of Hildesheim's Historia trium regum. It begins with prophecies and traditions surrounding the Magi, narrates their journey to Bethlehem and Adoration of Christ, follows their later conversion, preaching, deaths, and association with Saint Thomas, and traces the legendary transfer of their relics through Constantinople and Milan to Cologne. Long digressions describe Eastern lands, products, Christian communities, religious customs, Prester John, and the cult of the Three Kings.

3KCol.xml does not encode a simple single-witness transcription. It reorganizes Horstmann's parallel print edition into separate Cambridge and Royal divisions, retains his English variant apparatus, and omits the printed Latin text and marginal synopsis. The two main English divisions represent related recensions of one standard prose translation rather than two unrelated authored works.

notes: This is the established scholarly title for the standard prose translation, not a claim that all witnesses transmit an identical medieval title.
The qualifier distinguishes this translation from the independent Lambeth prose translation and the Middle English verse version.
titles.value: Historia trium regum. Middle English (standard prose version)

notes: This is the shorter form of Horstmann's edition title and the U-M item title, not necessarily an original title of the medieval translation.
titles.value: The Three Kings of Cologne: An Early English Translation of the 'Historia Trium Regum'

notes: Recorded as a witness title, not promoted to the sole preferred title of the cross-witness work.
